On what makes the growthx processes worth the ROI, compared to AIOps, like to me, correct me if I'm wrong, like even Joanne, I had these conversations around like, let's just assume you guys is one end of spectrum of like having super detailed like configurations and processes like AIOps in the middle, right?

think last year, you know, ChatGPT 3 and 4 were producing 50% of what I needed.

Okay.
Yeah, that's helpful to know.

And part of the reason I ask is, you know, Rodolfo, think like in situations where prospective clients are really interested in seeing what they can build, sometimes that is like the best first step, right, is to try that out so that you go through that process and really figure out if you can get to quality there before, right, like making a longer term investment with us.
To speak to your question, though, right, like what is differentiation in our process versus trying to go your own way?
I think one is the process of strategy first, execution second, and the artifacts being able to feed into the workflows that we create.
But I think...
You know, the other piece is how deep we can actually go with using like a custom knowledge base for you of your resources in addition to those artifacts and being strategic, right?
Like about the way that we might go and do the research for a certain assignment in addition to like all the fact checking that will go into every single passage and every article that we write.

We've created over 100 guides for AugmentCode.
All of these guides are highly technical.
We're comparing tools in this space.
We're, you know, serving as like a thought leader for them around AI coding.

The way that we're able to get that specific and get that focused is because not only do we have these artifacts here that go in detail around like their products and services and, you know, to your point, like you could create these artifacts yourself.
There's nothing magic about us doing it versus your team doing it.

We've got, you know, the products and services in addition to the personas and also this proofreader checklist that's being leveraged so that the content, not just from like brand guidelines, but from specific instructions is going to make sure that it solves for like all of these very specific requirements that they have around the sources that are used, around the citations.
And so, you know, these are the artifacts that are feeding into the workflows, but also there's this knowledge base.
And in this knowledge base, we've got all these different sources, right?
Some of them are like sources around research.
So founding history of Ruby on Rails, Mac OS and Next Step, Django.
But what's cool about this knowledge base is that we've got a knowledge graph to optimize for retrieval.

For the knowledge base integration.
And to that research.
So a couple of different things that we do up front on the context side that I thought would be helpful to start with.

that.

haven't really talked about that part of the process yet.

Maybe it's a step in the process, maybe it's the inputs that go in.

With the research step, you can see that we are giving very detailed instructions for this assignment from our team.

We're providing parameters and requirements around the course.
Content.

And so this is a lot of just the sophistication of the workflow, how high quality we can get the article.
To the question around our process, so our process in the strategy sprint, we do a kickoff call with you, and then we do weekly meetings, and that's it, right?
We're going to do our own deep research.
We're going to come prepared with specific discovery questions.
We're going to ingest resources from your team.
So if there are brand documents, if there are maybe a couple of transcripts from sales calls or a sales deck, these are all things.
That can help us move a lot faster.
If there's examples of content from your competitors that you really like or from your own content, this helps us calibrate faster.
And then when it comes to actually staging and publishing all the last mile execution, all we need is access to your CMS, right?
Like we've got forward deployed engineers, designers.
And so you don't have to do anything other than review the content that we draft, give feedback in a timely manner, and give a thumbs up when you're ready for us to publish.
We'll handle everything else.
So is that being handled by a human, the posting piece, or is that automation through AI?
Typically human, just logging in with credentials to your CMS.
If we can get to a step, if we can get to a point where we can automate it, we absolutely will.
We've automated publishing and staging and publishing in the CMS for a couple of our customers, but typically comes, you know, when we're at least three months in.
The engagement and feel really good about the operating cadence.
Go ahead.
I think, you know, Rodolfo, it's also the fact that it's not like we're assigning team members to accounts that have no background in content marketing or SEO, right?
Like you have a managing director, have a managing editor, you have a content manager, our chief content officer ran TechCrunch as editor-in-chief for over a decade.
And he oversees like all the work that's being done.
The platform allows the team to make changes to the content as it's being created.
So the expert is in the loop.
And what you'd get with us as a partner as well is someone who's always innovating, right?

We make this work today through like Airtable, Slack, Notion, Google Docs for reviewing the drafts.
But this will just continue to be like a partnership that evolves probably much faster than any other partner that you might be working with, with the technology that supports our delivery.
Any specific questions that I can go in detail around?
I'm trying to be helpful here.
I know it's a tough decision that you're thinking through.
Yeah.

How often does that happen?
Is that a regular occurrence?
Are they often in these pieces?

Yeah, that's a great question.

And there is, you know, circumstances where when they know a lot about how you want to sound and what the objective is, they make changes.
But I think it's something that changes during the review, right?
Like when we're calibrating, we're going to leverage the workflows quite a bit and see what you think.
And if you're having a lot of feedback, then we'll use that.
Feedback to like factor it in.
The goal is that nothing is put on your desk for review until a team member from our side has like read every word and tried to match the content to your standards.
There's more involvement at different times, so it's hard to answer, but it is by no means like put it through the workflows and whatever comes out is what we're going to put on your desk.
It's a combination and when you review, it informs a lot of how we, you know, use the workflows or use the editors to bring the content to that steady state.

It's like, or like they, they, they, they,
Didn't rank well in their category or like organic traffic didn't grow as fast as you wanted.

I would say like the biggest cause would be they came to us with no domain authority.
You know, they had unrealistic expectations around like the long-term investment that's needed to build an organic content engine.
And a lot of clients that have just published, you know, tens of pages versus hundreds or even a hundred as like, you know, a benchmark.
As you know, like takes time to start to build traction.
It takes publishing content daily for six plus months to, you know, start to see like a meaningful ROI.
And so I would say those are the clients who've been the hardest for us to work with.
And that's part of the reason why like series A plus is where.
Or maybe also the review process is super cumbersome.
They've got five different folks that want to weigh in on every piece and every person has a different point of view.
It's like when we can't get to quality fast enough and we can't have a streamlined way of staging and publishing.

I've had a really similar experience when I built a rapid content publishing engine at Amex for one of the B2B products and were able to sort of bypass legal review through this sort of self-checking, you know, like a self-made checklist that we agreed with our attorneys.
But when they went to shift it to another group, a larger product that the attorneys wanted to be involved, it sort of screeched to a halt.
So only, you know, the one B2B product got the benefit of rapid publication.
And hundreds of articles, like you're saying, versus having it be like a trickle effect.
Exactly.
Exactly.

mean, I think it's, like, more, as I've heard you say in the past, I think this would be most valuable in, like, a programmatic content play in terms of us making sure we continue to do this sort of daily and weekly publishing of um, um,
